motivation: Type signatures for functions that return nothing.
whyExists: Without **Void and Never**, modern software engineering would be extremely tedious. Developers would have to manually configure physical memory slots, write repetitive loop patterns, or establish unsafe socket connections for basic data operations. This concept exists to remove boilerplate redundancy, guarantee code safety limits, and offer a standard method for developers to manage application states.
realWorldAnalogy: Imagine you are working in a warehouse. Instead of tossing boxes around randomly, you place them into labeled shelves. When a package arrives, you look up its shelf label to update the stock index. Similarly, **Void and Never** acts as a named workspace or rule that coordinates statements, keeping variables safe and ensuring operations execute only when matching conditions are met.
simpleExplanation: To learn Void and Never from first principles, start by looking at how data resolves at runtime. Imagine your processor executing instructions sequentially. Void and Never defines how values bind, operations evaluate, and stack structures are clean.
visualDiagram:
```text
[User Input] ──► [Process Scope: Void and Never] ──► [Memory Address] ──► [Console Output]
```
syntaxExplanation: Verify that all brackets, colons, keywords, and assignments match the typescript syntax specifications. Avoid naming collisions by using scoped namespaces.

stepByStepExecution.desc: Allocate memory segment and load bindings.
